Sentence: You press the heel of your hand to the greenhouse pane and draw a rag through the cold bead of morning.
The streaks clear in an uneven arc; outside, the bay is a silver-gray band, a
barge a darker smudge on the horizon.
Your braid is warm against your neck, salt at the tips catching the light.
A few loose curls cling damp to your temples and spring away when you tilt
your head.
a pair of tide sensors, a damp pen, a waterproof notebook swollen with folded maps and pencil smudges.
You turn the notebook open with a thumb, watching a
Polaroid slide out half-stuck between pages and land on the workbench with a soft flutter.
Eli's grin looks like sunlight.
He is clay-specked and wind-whipped on the rooftop prototype — messy hair, sea-glass eyes that search
the frame as if he thinks the camera can still answer him.
proof that somebody besides you is still trying to build, to hold something in place.
You hear the phone on the bench — Tomas' voice, three calls in the span of an hour.
You answer with a practiced smile that doesn't reach your lungs.
Maya?
You awake?
Morning.
I was just—' (you gesture at the glass and condensation) '—wiping the panes.
What's up?
Couldn't sleep.
The tide's been all over the place.
Shop smells like diesel and your mother's coffee—God, I keep waking up thinking the floor's got salt under it.' (a dry chuckle that doesn't land) 'They're saying the channel markers are off.
I patched the back door last week but—
You did the right thing.
That's money and time saved if the surge comes.
Right thing.
Right thing.
Yeah.
Listen, Maya — if Aurelia comes in full tilt and they get their machines in, I'm not sure I can keep the rent.
They keep haggling over the buyout terms.
What if they push us?
What if they push everyone?
(you can feel a counterweight — all the rescue plans you draft in the greenhouse) 'Then we make a plan.
We map out the vulnerable units, we—'
Maps won't stop concrete cutters.' (pause; his voice softens) 'You always have a plan, sis.
I don't know if I do.
And I'm tired of being the one waiting for you to call with the 'next step' that's going to fix it all.
(you swallow) 'You're not waiting for me to fix it alone.
I'm working with Asha — and Rosa's holding the next meeting at the café.
We can push for the living beds, the shoreline modules.
But Tomas—' (your throat tightens) 'I can't promise it'll stop everything.'
I know.' (another long exhale) 'Just—call if you hear something.
If you get a notice or, I dunno, a drum of concrete shows up at the pier—you're the one people listen to.
I'll be at Tidewatch all morning.
I'll come by the shop after the meeting.' (you force the words out) 'Take pictures of anything suspicious.
Send them to me.
Okay.
Be careful, Maya.
Promise me you'll take a break today?
Eat something that isn't for the plants.
(a small, humorless smile) 'I'll try.
Love you.'
Love you too.
a steady, humid hush punctuated by the drip from the living beds.
The hydroponic basil smells bright and
almost absurdly domestic against the brine-stung air; its green is a straightness in a room full of leaning things.
You run a fingertip along the surface of a shallow trough and feel the cool slickness of
nutrient water.
Everything here feels like a promise you keep because you must.
You spread the notebook flat and begin to sketch contour lines for a proposed community garden that would act as a buffer — terraces of soil and root that slow water’s rush and keep the ground
from giving way.
Your pencil hesitates at each curve; the lines are careful because the places they mark are people's homes.
• Trace the contour with soft shading
You soften the lines with shaded pencil strokes, imagining the garden as a gentle spine that holds the neighborhood together.
The act calms you for a few breaths; guilt recedes to the edges.
• Draw bold retaining lines and label them
You press harder, delineating firm berms and anchor piles, writing capacities and materials in the margins.
The plan looks urgent, almost defensive; your chest tightens as responsibility shapes into technical demands.
Continue
You read Asha's notes again — her handwriting is a dense, looping architecture of assurance.
'Think like water,' she wrote in the margin of a page full of species lists, 'don't impose; redirect.' You say the
patient, a little prickly.
Think like water,
Water doesn't lose by changing.
It finds a new bed.
You can teach a shoreline to move with it — if you let it.
'But what if the town won't let it?
What if the money looks at a seawall and picks profit?”
Then you find the families who remember that pier.
You tell the story with salt on your tongue.
People listen to memory, Maya.
They listen to what they can lose.
You pause in the middle of the page, the pencil hovering.
The conversation is not a real exchange; Asha is not in the lab right now.
But you keep the dialogue because it steadies the tremor
that lives under your stern focus.
Her advice is a counterweight you can balance against the ledger-books and project bids that march through your inbox like a tide.
Outside, the barge doesn't move.
It is the color of old iron and has the slow inevitability of a thing someone has decided must happen.
Machines are not loud in this gray morning; they are patient.
The sight of it presses down somewhere behind your ribs, a weight that loves to make your mouth taste of old salt and sharpness.
Eli's Polaroid flutters when a draft comes in from the vent.
You pick it up again, fingers tracing the white border.
There is a small sticky note taped to the back in his messy handwriting Left a sample of the terracing tiles.
Saw a barge this morning.
Thinking of you — E.
You put the Polaroid into your vest pocket, over the tide sensor.
It feels like a small talisman.
• Clip the Polaroid to your notebook
You secure Eli's photo to the cover of the notebook so it will flutter out at the exact moment you need reminding.
The gesture sharpens your focus into a private promise.
• Leave the photo face-up on the bench
You leave the image on the bench where the light hits it.
It warms a small part of the lab like a single sunbeam and you let yourself watch it for a minute longer.
Continue
You go back to your sketches.
The calendar on the wall is a grid of meetings — Rosa at noon, Councilor Lin at three, a public forum scheduled for the week with Aurelia reps already slotted
where they intersect, the town's future might be scrawled out in someone else's ink.
you, seven, sitting at the end of the old pier with splinters in your palms, wind lifting a paper boat you’d made.
The storm that took it is a
a terrible, hollow wrenching.
You remember the
helplessness sticking to your scalp.
You rub the palm of your hand along the ridge of the notebook.
Guilt is a familiar ache, the one that keeps you awake stitching plans together until dawn.
It is the engine beneath every contour
line you draw.
You tell yourself that the guilt is useful — a fuel — but it tastes like rust today.
The greenhouse breathes around you.
Your shoulders slump, involuntarily small.
There is no dramatic thunderclap here; there is only the patient, very real collapse of so many small things into that low, ongoing loss.
The morning
presses its grayness into the glass until even the edges of Eli’s smile seem softened.
You stand up and walk to the nearest pane.
The barge seems a little closer, though it might be the way the light has shifted.
You trace a slow line across the fogged glass with your fingertip, as if you can redraw the horizon.
You breathe in, feeling the humid air fill your chest, and then let it out.
The lab is a small island of green against a growing gray outside.
It doesn't feel like enough, and the thought of 'not enough' sits heavy and plain in your throat.
You gather your things with deliberate slowness.
Meetings will stack into one another like the tide; obligations will arrive somber and inevitable.
You fold Asha's notes into your notebook, clip the pen into your field vest, and shoulder the weight of the day.
A gull cries far away.
The drip from the living beds counts the seconds.
to hold in place what the water would take.
The reminder is both anchor and chain.
You step toward the door, letting the humid air cling to your braid.
The morning is a slow, heavy tide pulling at everything you have promised to protect.
You walk the length of Main Street with the brass compass against your thigh, its small weight a metronome against the rhythm in your chest.
The harbor smells of kelp and diesel and the cinnamon warmth
spilling from Rosa’s café; steam threads the air into small, honest clouds.
Your braid clings damp against your neck.
The morning is neither bright nor dark — washed in the gray that has become this town’s
normal — and it presses you into a careful, folding quiet.
There you are, niña.
You look like you slept with charts in your brain.
You try to smile.
'I wished I had,' you say, and the words land smaller than you expect.
How is Tomas?
He’s...
considering options.
He’s stubborn in the way that makes me proud and terrified.
Rosa squeezes your hand.
The café’s air is thick with roasted beans and cardamom; the string of fairy lights in the window casts halos on her wrinkled smile.
Tell him for me — whatever he decides, there'll always be coffee here.' (beat) 'And tell him to stop using my tables for welding, eh?
• Say, 'I'll tell him, but he might need convincing.'
You promise in a softer voice, picturing Tomas's stubborn jaw, and Rosa nods like she already knows.
• Say, 'I'll bring him by myself.'
The offer trembles out of you; Rosa's eyes go bright and old and grateful, and you feel the first small lift of not carrying everything alone.
a low brick building whose steps have been smoothed by a hundred meetings,
a hundred weeks where people came together to decide how to keep their homes intact.
Good.
Now go.
Eyes open, Maya.
Under Marco's umbrella, he arrives precisely on schedule, the blazer more immaculate than the weather allows.
He moves through the crowd with the night-side confidence of someone practiced at small shows of control.
Good morning, everyone.
Thank you for coming.
His greeting is a thin shield.
You can see the way some faces relax and others tighten.
Marco's vote is the hinge; he knows it, they all know it.
You feel Asha's presence like an anchor at your back — quiet in a way that cuts through the room's noise.
We should listen to every proposal fully,' she says to you as she joins, voice like worn sea glass.
'The science needs to be heard, but the people must be seen.
You nod.
Her certainty is small and steady; it keeps you tethered beneath the tilt of your worry.
trench coat clasped, briefcase clicked closed.
The silver streak at her temple catches the light like a punctuation.
Her presence cuts a different sort of certainty—engineered, polished, no room for fog.
Thank you, Councilor Lin, and thank you, San Brisa,' she begins, voice even and practiced.
'Aurelia's seawall is a proven solution.
We have modeled storm resistance, insurance reductions, and timelines that protect your property values immediately.
expected wave heights, cost-benefit analyses, insurance-savings charts.
The language is clinical.
'Protection,' 'mitigation,' 'efficiency.'
You feel your notebook grow heavier in your hands, though it's unchanged; the weight is a thought that sits in your chest.
Words slide into you — 'immediate,' 'certainty' — and though you know the numbers,
a blunt edge between people and ocean, a boundary that keeps some things safe by divorcing others.
Eli Navarro is there, sleeves rolled as if he'd been pulled from a workbench and set in the front row.
His paint-speckled fingers are as you remembered, tucking a folding timber ruler into a back pocket
on instinct.
When June concludes, he stands with the easy gravity you have come to rely on.
Clinically tidy, yes,' he says, voice warm but not without edge.
'But we need to think about the living systems those pylons replace.
Living shorelines—marsh restoration, engineered reefs—absorb energy and preserve access.
They’re not just ecological ornament; they’re long-term resilience.
Mr.
Navarro, with respect, living systems don't provide the same immediate, quantifiable protection for high-value commercial zones.
Time matters when banks demand certainty and insurers calculate premiums.
And time also matters when you push people out of neighborhoods to save profit centers.
We aren't asking to ignore immediate needs.
We're proposing phased pilot projects that demonstrate function — we can model for insurers and show staged results.
Pilot projects are costly and uncertain.
Aurelia's approach secures the town now.
Secures, or shifts risk elsewhere?' 'We can design prototypes that are quicker and cheaper than you assume.
We have skilled people here.
We can prove it.
June's mouth thins.
The exchange is measured but the subtext is sharp.
Around you, murmurs ripple like a distant tide—approval and skepticism braided together.
• Interject, citing Asha's field data.
You open your mouth and then close it; Asha gives you a small look that says 'later.' You swallow and let Eli take the front line for now.
• Hold your ground, let Eli speak while you watch Tomas.
You watch Eli frame the arguments, and your eyes find Tomas in the doorway.
His jaw is tight, the corner of his mouth set.
Letting others speak feels like an exercise in trust and dread.
Tomas stands at the room's threshold like a hinge.
He doesn't enter fully; he lets the door frame hold him.
His hands are the hands that fixed your mother's porch, the hands that steady the town's
small machinery.
The decision today is not just policy — it's a ledger of the life he keeps.
You think of Rosa's coffee-stained smile, of Asha's patient insistence, of Eli's easy gestures.
The room divides between those who want solidity now and those who wager on a different future.
Every proposal reads like a promise and a threat at once.
Both proposals raise valid points.
The question facing us is which path protects our town and its people in the short and mid term.' 'I want to hear from the community.
Let us proceed with questions.
June Park nods, composed; Eli Navarro leans forward, elbows splayed, ready.
Voices that had been murmurs step into the room like the slow climbing of a tide — careful, edged with worry.
You feel your pulse shift from a steady thrum to a quiet, urgent tapping.
It is not loud enough to startle anyone; it is only a reminder under the skin.
The options in the room are
not theoretical anymore.
You picture Tomas's shop window, the salt-etched tools, the list of customers pinned beneath a magnet.
remember the data, remember the people.
You prepare to speak — to thread the numbers with the memories, to make the scientific plausible and the human present — and the room leans in.
The decision is about to be framed; the first public opinion will land like a stone.
You stand on the edge of that moment, feeling all the small things that have led here — guilt, hope,
a tether of loyalty to a town that has given you everything and asked for too much in return.
You are ready to speak.
The room waits.
You stand beneath the projector's wash, the light thinning the lines around your eyes into a map of fatigue.
The hum presses gently at the base of your skull like an approaching tide.
Outside, on the
slide, Aurelia's cranes are already staged — bright floodlights stabbed into the dusk — and for a moment the construction looks less like a plan and more like a promise of permanence.
The air tastes faintly of metal and coffee.
Someone's umbrella drip puddles by the door; Rosa's shawl smells of cardamom and old wood.
You can feel salt at the back of your throat even though you're
inside — a memory of the harbor, of boards that warp and sigh when storms come.
Your brass compass, warm from your palm, sits on the table between you and the council like a small, stubborn
star.
You set the compass on the table with careful fingers.
It is ridiculous and necessary, both.
Ridiculous because it's only brass and glass; necessary because it is a thing that points when everything else argues.
The room leans in.
You clear your throat.
Maya.
The floor is yours.
June Park watches you with an even patience that doesn't need to hurry.
Her gray eyes are polished and precise; the rest of her is tied up in a neat trench coat and a briefing packet.
She has the kind of composure that reads as competence in every press photograph you've seen.
Now, in the same room as your neighbors, it reads as certainty.
Securing immediate loss is— (she chooses the words like placing stones) —our priority.
Aurelia's seawall will buy the town time.
It will protect Main Street, the commercial strip.
Data supports it.
Eli leans forward beside you, one elbow on the table.
His sea-glass eyes are softer than June's, searching for the seam where your resolve might fray so he can hold it.
We can't pretend this isn't urgent.
But urgency doesn't have to mean one fixed tool.
Living edges can be deployed in tandem; we can prototype and protect.
You feel Asha's presence as a small, steady pressure at your back — the weight of someone who has mapped tides with a pencil and walked spits of land while sea levels rose a fingertip each
year.
Rosa's hands fold on the table like an anchor; her mouth turns at the corners with worry that tastes like memory.
Buyouts are in the memo.
I need to know if I sell the shop, sis.
If council votes for Aurelia, I don't get a choice.
I—
Your pulse is a quiet metronome.
Look at the room.
See how people are not just a list of stakeholders but a mesh of lives — a neighbor whose porch will flood first, a young couple
with a baby in the next block, an old man who remembers the pier that used to sing its boards in summer.
You are not arguing numbers; you are arguing futures.
cross-sections of a seawall, a living shoreline, cost estimates, and a smear of Aurelia's logo.
The title at the top reads Protecting San Brisa: Immediate Solutions.
• Begin with hard data — charts and thresholds
You let the numbers speak first, voice measured.
You point to the projected storm-surge line and the model timelines; people nod, some with relief, some with guarded faces.
• Start with a memory — Rosa’s story of the old pier
its boards, the way the tide would sing.
Heads turn; for a second the municipal charts lose their tyranny and the room remembers what’s at stake.
the memory to anchor attention, the numbers to give it shape.
Your voice finds an even cadence.
You choose — and the room shifts under that choice, just slightly.
If you led with data, you feel validation ripple through the planners; if you led with memory, eyes soften and the numbers have faces.
the memory to anchor attention, the numbers to give it shape.
Your voice finds an even cadence.
Engineered protection can secure commerce quickly, but it also severs ecological connections that communities rely on.
A seawall shifts wave energy.
It changes where fish spawn, where marsh can migrate.
Those are not abstract losses — they are losses that feed our tables and our stories.
And if we wait, Maya — if we bet on slowly implemented pilots — people will lose houses this season.
Aurelia moves at scale.
We can put steel where water is taking wood, and buy time for residents.
June's tone is not belligerent; it is exact, like a calculation.
You respect the clarity.
But the town is not a calculation only.
Eli touches the back of your hand — a light, private contact meant to steady and to say We are here together.
His fingers smell faintly of clay and the salt from his pockets.
• Let Eli's touch steady you
The contact is a small, warm anchor and you draw breath.
Your voice even now, you can feel his patience pulse against your skin.
• Pull your hand back and keep professional distance
You fold your hands in front of you and make a space between the personal and the procedural.
Eli's mouth quirks, and the room feels a touch colder — focused.
• Squeeze his hand briefly, then let go
You give his fingers a brief, private squeeze — acknowledgment and a promise — then return your attention to the council.
His eyes lift; there is a question there without words.
You let small human things thread through the meeting because this is not a courtroom of abstractions — it's your town.
You let small human things thread through the meeting because this is not a courtroom of abstractions — it's your town.
Asha Malik adjusts her glasses and slides a packet across the table to Councilor Marco.
Her voice is calm, precise.
We can model phased living-shoreline deployments.
They reduce erosion, support biodiversity, and can be staged incrementally.
The upfront risk is lower than people fear when we coordinate evacuation plans and emergency funding.
Rosa leans in, voice husky with years and coffee When they put up the last seawall in my cousin's town, the fish simply moved out.
The kids had no place to learn the bay.
We cannot put a fence between us and the water and call that protection.
Aurelia's proposal includes adaptive elements.
The wall is engineered with sensors.
It will be maintained.
If you work with us, we can include ecological offsets.
Offsets are not the same as habitat.
There's also the legal and political power differential; Aurelia can outspend us on monitoring and lobbyists.
The community's knowledge of place — Rosa's histories, Tomas's storefront life — that's irreplaceable.
So, what then?
People wait for prototypes and the buyouts keep happening in the meantime?
I have rent, bills.
Compromise means my family doesn't get left behind.
There is a tightness now under the ribs of the room.
The hum of the projector feels louder by contrast, time compressed.
You take another breath, tasting salt and metal and roasted coffee; your mouth goes dry.
We've heard data, lived testimony, and corporate plans.
The motion before us is to decide a town path.
Maya, you've been at the center of the community plan — we need your recommendation.
Your compass sits between you and the council like a miniature star.
You know where it points; you know what you fear.
You also know that every choice trades one set of risks for another.
You step forward.
The light on your face makes the hollows beneath your cheekbones shallow; you can see your reflection in the polished surface of the projector like a faint ghost.
An engineered seawall as the only option risks ecological collapse of the shoreline and, in the mid- to long-term, greater costs and displacement.
A living-shoreline pilot buys us adaptive learning and community ownership.
A legal/media campaign can expose Aurelia's risks and pressure better terms.
A negotiated hybrid could harness immediate protection while reserving rights for ecological interventions.
Those are three very different paths.
Each has trade-offs.
Which do you advise we choose?
not a dramatic showdown, but a municipal decision with real people on either side.
Tomas's freckled hands, Rosa's coffee-stained apron, Asha's worn field jacket, Eli's hopeful smile.
You consider the image of Aurelia's floodlights cutting the harbor into a foreign geography and the living plans that might knit back what is fraying.
organizer,
litigator, or broker.
Each will shift alliances, test trust, and ask neighbors to make very personal bets.
The air tastes metallic.
Your mouth is cotton-dry.
The compass seems to tick, not with time, but with possibility.
Maya Reyes — we ask you to recommend a path forward for San Brisa.
You inhale.
You think of measured timelines and immediate storms, of livelihoods and histories.
You glance at Eli; his eyes are asking not for a vote but for faith.
You glance at June; her face is
unreadable, composed.
Rosa's hands are folded in an old rhythm of prayer and paperwork.
Tomas's jaw is set; fear and pride in close quarters.
You are at the fulcrum.
You stand beneath the glare and it feels like the sun has been replaced by a practical, indifferent bulb.
The folders make a soft, papery cliff at your elbow; Asha's notes are clipped to the top.
Your brass compass sits on the first exhibit — heavy and familiar, a small object that refuses to let your hands forget the horizon.
Read this sentence aloud,' she says, voice steady as tidewater.
'Not for the court—out loud, like a person, not a report.
If I let it sound like a paper, they file and move on.
If I make it sound like a neighbor, it becomes harder to ignore.
Always choose the neighbor.
Her counsel steadies you; she holds the quiet, clinical patience of someone who has measured the slow decline of reefs and knows the difference between hysteria and evidence.
Around you, courthouse staff move in no-nonsense lines.
Cameras will come later; for now there are only forms, signatures, and the hum of official motion.
this is not a prototype you can build and test; it is a narrative you must defend under cross-examination.
• Reread the affidavit one more time
You flip the page, voice quieter, finding the sentence you know will make someone in the room think of their grandmother's garden.
The words settle into your mouth like a stone.
• Trust Asha and sign
You lay the pen down and let Asha take the lead.
Signing feels like handing the compass to tide and asking it to point true.

Asha leans in, lowering her voice We can file for the temporary injunction this morning.
It will buy time — not forever, but enough to test the models, to get a hearing before they pour more concrete.
a court order slows a machine that is otherwise impossible to halt.
That hope hums in your chest like an engine.
The journalist you coordinated with approaches, not hostile, but sharp — a blade softened enough to slice paper, not bone.
He speaks in quick, decisive lines that force answers into small spaces.
Journalist Ms.
Reyes—what proof do you have that Aurelia's construction threatens the marsh now, not later?
Is this delay worth the economic cost?
We have peer-reviewed assessments, before-and-after sediment samples, and clear photographic documentation of erosion coinciding with the construction's tidal shadow.
Journalist And do you expect the court to stop construction?
We expect the court to recognize immediate risk and grant a temporary hold until an impartial review is completed.
This isn't about stopping progress — it's about making progress that doesn't erase us.
June Park watches, smile as polished as the briefcase at her side.
When the microphone swings to her, she speaks in the same clear, composed tone that has carried her through investor pitches and town halls.
We welcome scrutiny.
Aurelia follows regulations and has always prioritized safety.
Our seawall is an engineering solution to an urgent problem.
You feel a flare of irritation — not at her words, but at the way her assurance is built from polished metal and shareholder charts rather than the soft math of marshes.
Still, the crowd hums;
scrutiny brings accountability.
That thought lifts you.
Eli Navarro steps back, camera at his shoulder, framing the construction site on the far edge of town.
He hands you a warm Polaroid — his fingers leave a faint smudge of developer chemicals.
The picture
a strip of mud marred by tire tracks, a pool of oily sheen where a storm drain discharges.
You wanted proof.
I'm getting it.
We get these into the filings and into the papers.
Careful with who we show.
Some folks won't want their homes photographed.
Then we'll get close-ups of the damage.
Not people, just the scars.
• Stand where the cameras can see you
You step forward, letting the microphone find your face.
The crowd leans in; the camera lenses make your jawline look sharper than it feels.
Your voice carries better than you expected.
• Stay beside Eli, out of frame
You stay to the side, letting the images speak.
You're relieved when Eli gives you a look that says he understands the risks.
The Polaroid in your hand warms like a pact.

You move through the site with Eli, boots sinking slightly in churned mud where the tide still remembers how to resist.
He snaps shot after shot.
His camera pauses on the edge of the marsh where
the construction's shadow falls like a blade.
You press your palm to the damp earth and feel a cool, sullen resistance.
Tomas meets you near the temporary fencing, cap low, fingers worrying at the edge of a paper cup.
His shop is less than a block away — the rent his whole life balances on.
You sure about making noise, Maya?
Folks are getting buyout offers left and right.
If Aurelia sniffs a chance, they'll start sweet-talking people out of their houses.
The point is to keep people from being forced into decisions they don't have to make yet.
If the court slows them down, we have time to organize—negotiate on fair terms or stop the worst of it.
Organize?
Organize's a luxury when you can't keep the lights on.
Rosa joins you, apron still wrapped around her waist even though she's not at the café.
Her voice is small but clear.
You are risking gossip, niña.
People will pick sides.
They will call you trouble if it means someone keeps their rent.
translate data into neighbor-stories.
Make the court see faces.
We're not making enemies of our neighbors.
We're trying to make their neighbors safer.
• Promise Tomas you'll prioritize his shop in the campaign
You tell him you'll highlight his concerns in the filings and make sure any relocation talk includes fair compensation and time.
Tomas nods, half-convinced, the worry lines softening.
• Tell Tomas this is bigger than any one shop
some things are bigger.
He looks away, a quick flinch of hurt.
For a second the two of you are strangers again.

Eli Navarro crouches, framing a shot of a displaced marsh crab clinging to a blade of grass.
He looks up, and for the first time since this started, his voice tightens.
People will see this.
If we get the right spread, the council can't pretend this is abstract anymore.
You want to believe him.
His hope is a combustible fuel — optimistic, maybe naive, but it ignites the room.
The more eyes that watch June's site, the harder it is for the company to move without accountability.
a marker that promises the law will make someone look.

The clerk hands you a copy with an official scrawl.
When's the hearing?

Emergency docket moved it up.
strategy meetings, press briefings, depositions.
some clap quietly for you; others avert their faces or mutter words you don't catch.
Rosa squeezes your hand; her knuckles are warm.
We pray, we plan, and we keep our doors open.
Law slows machines.
Science gives reasons.
People give the story.
Together, they make an argument no one can ignore.
You breathe that in.
The arousal that has been a rising swell — the pacing of meetings, the click of cameras, the staccato of legal action — reaches a sharp pitch.
It's frightening and electric.

pressure, yes, but pressure that could reshape the hull of the ship.
June Park crosses the plaza toward you, past camera glare and murmured questions.
There is a flicker of real emotion under her composed surface — a quick hardening around her mouth, like someone bracing for impact.
Ms.
Reyes.
I respect your right to challenge.
But consider the cost of delay — small businesses, homes that need protection now.
Protection that damages the marsh will cost the town more in the long run.
We're asking for due process.
We both want to protect San Brisa.
We just read the map differently.
There's no villainy in her words — only an honest conviction that engineering equals safety.
The public exchange is polite and jagged.
Cameras drink it in.
Your heart beats its loud, escalating rhythm.
Eli Navarro finds you then, hand on your shoulder, his eyes bright.
They printed our pictures.
Front page.
